FT_IC1.evidence: US ABCD Study, baseline n=10,116, 2-year follow-up n=7,880

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time (self-reported, total hours on weekdays/weekends for video games + TV watching) qualifies under B7 carve-out as integrated measure; applies B1 (screen time has separate coefficient from brain structure outcomes)

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear mixed-effects regression models testing screen time against ADHD symptoms (β=0.032, p=0.001 at baseline) and cortical thickness outcomes; mediation analysis using bias-corrected bootstrap; multiple inferential tests across brain regions

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple extractable coefficients: screen time→ADHD (β=0.032, p=0.001); cortical thickness effects (right temporal pole β=-0.036 FDR p=0.020; left superior frontal gyrus β=-0.028 FDR p=0.020; left rostral middle frontal gyrus β=-0.030 FDR p=0.020); mediation effect (β=0.001, p=0.023)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD empirical analysis examining screen time associations with ADHD symptoms and brain structure development

FT_IC6.evidence: No indication of duplicate/preprint; published 2025 in Translational Psychiatry

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time is the INDEPENDENT variable predicting ADHD symptoms (non-SM outcome) and brain structure; FT-EC7 only applies when SM/smartphone is the DEPENDENT variable in ALL analyses with demographic-only predictors. This paper has SM/screen-as-IV→outcome direction, disqualifying FT-EC7.
notes: Linear mixed-effects model employed; longitudinal design with residualized change regression; mediation analysis with 10,000 bootstrap samples; FDR multiple-comparison correction applied

FT_IC1.evidence: US ABCD Study data analyzed (adolescent sample from multiple US sites)

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time (bedtime/evening use on weekdays and weekends) qualifies under B7; measured separately with own coefficients in SEM model (Cohen's d effect sizes reported for SM use contrasts)

FT_IC3.evidence: Structural equation modeling (SEM) examining screen time associations with chronotype, social jet lag, and sleep duration; t-tests for group contrasts (Hispanic vs. non-Hispanic); SEM path coefficients for predictor relationships

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple extractable numeric coefficients: Cohen's d values for screen time differences (weekday evenings d=0.27, weekend evenings d=0.35); SEM model variance explained (chronotype 12.9%, social jet lag 10.5%, sleep duration 6.2%); t-test statistics reported

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD empirical analysis of screen time and sleep outcomes with demographic covariates

FT_IC6.evidence: Published 2025 in Sleep Advances; no indication of duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time is an INDEPENDENT variable predicting sleep outcomes (non-SM outcomes: chronotype, social jet lag, weekday sleep duration); additional non-demographic predictors included (peer network health, school environment, stress, depression, anxiety). FT-EC7 requires SM as DV with demographic-only predictors—this paper has screen time as IV with behavioral/psychological predictors, disqualifying FT-EC7.
notes: SEM with demographic covariates and additional behavioral/psychological predictors; Hispanic ethnicity moderation tested; paper notes methodological limitations including low caffeine use and assessment timing variability

FT_IC1.evidence: US ABCD Study cohort study, baseline n=4,285 (subset of 11,868), ages 9-10 years, 21 US sites, 4-year follow-up

FT_IC2.evidence: Addictive use of social media (SMAQ), mobile phones (MPIQ), and video games (VGAQ)—all qualify as smartphone/SM variables per B1. Separate coefficients reported for each platform and trajectory. Binding rule B1 applies: SMAQ/MPIQ/VGAQ papers with separate β/OR/RR reported regardless of total screen time also measured.

FT_IC3.evidence: Latent class linear mixed models to identify trajectories; Poisson regression models estimating risk ratios (RRs) for suicidal behaviors/ideation; generalized linear models for internalizing/externalizing symptoms; multiple-comparison FDR correction

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple extractable RR coefficients: increasing SM addictive use RR=2.14 [95% CI 1.61-2.85] for suicidal behaviors; high-peaking SM RR=2.39 [95% CI 1.66-3.43]; high video game trajectory T-score difference 2.03 [95% CI 1.45-2.61] for internalizing symptoms; increasing SM T-score difference 1.05 [95% CI 0.54-1.56] for externalizing symptoms

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD empirical longitudinal analysis (years 2-4 follow-up) examining addictive use trajectories and mental health outcomes

FT_IC6.evidence: Published 2025 in JAMA; no indication of duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: Social media/mobile phones are INDEPENDENT variables predicting suicidal behaviors, suicidal ideation, and internalizing/externalizing symptoms (non-SM outcomes); FT-EC7 requires SM as the DEPENDENT variable in EVERY analysis. This paper has SM→outcome direction (inverse of FT-EC7 requirement), disqualifying it. Additionally, clinical covariates (baseline suicidal behaviors, suicidal ideation, internalizing/externalizing symptoms) beyond demographics are included in models, further disqualifying FT-EC7.
notes: Latent class linear mixed models based on age and quadratic age terms; trajectories identified for social media (3 classes: high-peaking 9.6%, increasing 31.3%, low 59.1%), mobile phones (3 classes), and video games (2 classes); E-values computed for sensitivity to unmeasured confounding; FDR multiple-test correction via Benjamini-Hochberg
